content.text: Every call to a language model is a
stateless function

— the model has no persistent memory, no access to databases, and no awareness of time beyond its training cutoff. Whatever background knowledge, real-time state, or prior conversation a task requires must be
assembled and injected

into the prompt by the orchestrating code before inference begins. Context and input data are the evidence the model reads; their quality, ordering, and labeling directly control whether the model reasons accurately or hallucinates.
What it is
In an agentic prompt,
context

refers to all supporting information the model needs to interpret and act on the current task: prior conversation turns, retrieved knowledge chunks, tool call outputs, running state, user-profile fields, and static policy documents.
Input data

is the primary payload for this specific turn — the text, file contents, or structured record the task asks the model to read, transform, or reason about. The distinction matters: context is background; input data is the foreground.
The two are often delivered in the same message but serve different roles. Context answers the question
"what should the model already know before it starts?"
while input data answers
"what is the model being asked to work on right now?"
Blurring this distinction — for example, embedding the task instruction inside a long context blob — leads to instruction drift and unreliable outputs.
Common context types in agentic systems
Conversation history
— prior user and assistant turns that establish what has already been discussed, decided, or clarified. Typically time-ordered, often summarized or truncated.
Retrieval chunks (RAG)
— passages pulled from a vector store or search engine whose embeddings are close to the query. High-precision grounding for knowledge-intensive tasks.
Tool / API results
— JSON, text, or structured data returned by tool calls in the current or earlier steps. Always fresher than the model's weights.
Static reference documents
— schemas, policy files, API specs, coding guidelines, or domain handbooks that do not change per request. Often cached in the system prompt.
State and metadata
— plan steps completed, open todos, session variables, active feature flags, locale, timestamp, and user-preference fields.
Memory summaries
— compact representations of older turns compressed by a summarization step to stay within the context budget.
What counts as input data
The user's current natural-language message or command.
A file, code snippet, or document the user has uploaded for this request.
A structured record (JSON, CSV row, form submission) the agent is asked to classify, extract, or transform.
The diff, patch, or test failure the agent is asked to analyze.
The search query, database row, or API response that arrived from the previous pipeline stage.
Why it matters
Agents fail in two predictable ways when context is mishandled.
Hallucination
occurs when the model lacks grounding data and fills the gap with plausible-sounding fiction — fabricated citations, invented API parameters, or nonexistent company policies.
Stale reasoning

occurs when outdated context dominates the window: the model acts on an old tool result or a summarized turn that lost a critical detail, despite a fresh answer sitting elsewhere in the prompt. Both failure modes are context problems, not intelligence problems.

Context windows are a finite, expensive resource. A 128k-token context at GPT-4o-class pricing costs on the order of $0.40–$1.30 per call. Every token spent on irrelevant history is a token unavailable for instructions, schemas, or the actual input. Careful context management therefore has direct cost and latency implications for production systems running thousands of agent calls per hour.

The ordering and labeling of context also determines how reliably the model can locate and use it. Research into the "lost-in-the-middle" effect shows that models preferentially attend to content near the beginning and end of long contexts; important facts buried in the middle of an undifferentiated block of text are frequently ignored even when they are present.
Benefits
Factual grounding
— answers cite supplied evidence rather than relying on frozen training-time knowledge, dramatically reducing hallucination rates on private or recent data.
Tool alignment
— freshly injected tool results steer the next reasoning step using actual API state rather than the model's guess about what the API might return.
Session continuity
— multi-turn workflows preserve necessary decisions and user preferences across calls without replaying the entire raw transcript.
Auditability
— logged context shows exactly what information the model had access to when it produced an output, enabling post-hoc debugging and compliance checks.
Domain adaptation
— injecting company policies, internal schemas, or domain handbooks gives a general-purpose model specialized knowledge without fine-tuning.
Composability
— different agents in a pipeline can share a structured context bundle, passing state forward without exposing raw model internals.
Trade-offs
Context length vs. cost and latency
— doubling context roughly doubles inference cost and adds non-trivial latency. There is no free "add everything" solution.
Recall vs. precision in retrieval
— fetching more chunks improves recall but pollutes the window with loosely related text that degrades precision. Top-3 to top-10 retrieved chunks is a common practical range.
Summarization vs. fidelity
— compressing old turns preserves budget but can silently drop edge-case constraints the user stated early in a long session.
Static context vs. staleness
— injecting reference docs at the system-prompt level enables prompt caching (significant cost savings) but means the model won't see documents updated after the cache was set.
Unlabeled tool JSON vs. model confusion
— pasting raw API responses without a source label or type annotation causes smaller models to conflate tool output with user instructions or prior conversation.
How it works
Before each model call the orchestrating code runs a
context assembly pipeline

: a sequence of selection, ranking, budgeting, and formatting steps that converts raw data sources into a structured prompt. The pipeline is deterministic code, not model reasoning — it runs on the host before any tokens are sent to the model API.
The five-stage assembly pipeline
Select

sources relevant to this turn. For retrieval, this means embedding the current query and fetching the top-k nearest chunks. For history, this means choosing the most recent N turns or summarizing older ones.
Score and rank

each candidate block. Retrieval chunks are ranked by semantic similarity score. Tool results are ranked by recency and relevance to the pending sub-task. Redundant or duplicate content is deduplicated.
Allocate the token budget

across source types. A typical allocation policy reserves a fixed number of tokens per category (e.g., 8k for retrieval, 4k for tool results, 6k for history) and discards low-priority content that does not fit.
Format and delimit
every block with a consistent structural label so the model can parse source types without ambiguity. XML tags or markdown headers work well.
Inject in stable order

— system instructions first, static reference second, retrieved context next, tool results after, conversation history near the end, current input last. Consistent ordering improves reproducibility and exploits recency bias for the current task.
The total available context can be expressed as a simple budget constraint. If
is the maximum context window in tokens and
is the number of tokens reserved for the model's output, the available input budget is:
where
is the fixed cost of the system prompt. The remaining budget
is divided across retrieval, tool results, history, and the current task input. Each category gets a cap
such that:
When a source exceeds its cap, it is truncated, summarized, or dropped in reverse priority order — low-relevance history first, low-score retrieval chunks second, and never the current input task.
Architecture

The diagram below shows the full context assembly pipeline. Raw sources on the left feed into an orchestrator-side assembler, which applies selection, ranking, budget allocation, and formatting before producing the structured prompt. The prompt sections appear in a stable order — system instructions at the top, current input task anchored at the bottom. The token budget bar illustrates a representative 32k-token allocation across source types.

attrs.alt: Context and input data assembly pipeline diagram

content.text: When to use it

Include context whenever the task depends on information not guaranteed to be in model weights: private company data, real-time API state, user-specific history, or time-sensitive facts. A model answering questions about a SaaS product's current pricing, open support tickets, or internal HR policies cannot rely on its training — this information must be injected.

Omit context (or use minimal context) only for fully self-contained transformations where the task input is entirely sufficient on its own. Examples: rewriting a paragraph for tone, translating a sentence, generating a UUID, or formatting a date string. Adding unnecessary context for these tasks wastes tokens and can paradoxically confuse the model by suggesting there is background it should incorporate.
Decision checklist
Does the task require knowledge the model could not have learned before its training cutoff? → inject retrieval context.
Does the task depend on the result of an API call made in this session? → inject tool results.
Does correct behavior depend on what the user said earlier in this conversation? → inject conversation history.
Does the task need to comply with a proprietary schema, policy, or style guide? → inject static reference documents.
Is the task a pure text transformation using only the text supplied as input? → skip extra context.
Is this turn in a multi-step plan where earlier steps produced state? → inject the running state object.
How to implement
A robust context assembly layer is structured code, not ad-hoc string concatenation. The implementation pattern below uses a
ContextBundle
class that holds source slots with individual token caps, assembles them in priority order, and serializes to a prompt string. Token counting uses the model's actual tokenizer (via
tiktoken
for OpenAI models) to avoid off-by-thousands errors.
Install a tokenizer library matching your model provider (tiktoken for GPT models, anthropic's token-counter for Claude, or a generic byte-pair encoder).
Define a ContextBundle dataclass with named slots (system, retrieval, tools, history, input) and per-slot token budgets.
Fill each slot from its source: run a vector search for retrieval, collect the last N tool results for tools, apply a sliding-window or summarization strategy for history.
Call bundle.assemble() to serialize to a prompt string with consistent XML delimiters and slot labels.
Log the assembled bundle token count per slot for observability.
Pass the assembled string (or structured messages list) to the model API.

# context_bundle.py — reusable context assembly layer
import tiktoken
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class ContextSlot:
    name: str
    content: str
    max_tokens: int
    priority: int  # lower = higher priority; never drop priority 0

@dataclass
class ContextBundle:
    model: str = "gpt-4o"
    total_budget: int = 28_000  # leave room for output + system

    _slots: list[ContextSlot] = field(default_factory=list)

    def add(self, name: str, content: str, max_tokens: int, priority: int = 5) -> "ContextBundle":
        self._slots.append(ContextSlot(name, content, max_tokens, priority))
        return self

    def _count(self, text: str) -> int:
        enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model(self.model)
        return len(enc.encode(text))

    def _truncate(self, text: str, max_tokens: int) -> str:
        enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model(self.model)
        tokens = enc.encode(text)
        if len(tokens) <= max_tokens:
            return text
        return enc.decode(tokens[:max_tokens]) + "\n[...truncated]"

    def assemble(self) -> str:
        remaining = self.total_budget
        sections: list[tuple[int, str]] = []

        for slot in sorted(self._slots, key=lambda s: s.priority):
            cap = min(slot.max_tokens, remaining)
            trimmed = self._truncate(slot.content, cap)
            actual = self._count(trimmed)
            remaining -= actual
            sections.append((slot.priority, f"<{slot.name}>\n{trimmed}\n</{slot.name}>"))
            if remaining <= 0:
                break

        # Reassemble in original slot order (not priority order) for stable prompt layout
        ordered_names = [s.name for s in self._slots]
        name_to_text = {s.name: text for _, text in sections
                        for s in self._slots if f"<{s.name}>" in text}

        parts = []
        for name in ordered_names:
            if name in name_to_text:
                parts.append(name_to_text[name])

        return "\n\n".join(parts)


# ----- Usage -----
bundle = ContextBundle(model="gpt-4o", total_budget=28_000)

bundle.add(
    name="retrieved_knowledge",
    content="[Doc 1] Refund policy: items must be returned within 30 days...\n[Doc 2] Shipping FAQ: standard delivery takes 3-5 business days...",
    max_tokens=8_000,
    priority=2,
)
bundle.add(
    name="tool_result",
    content='{"tool": "lookup_order", "call_id": "c-42", "result": {"order_id": "A991", "status": "shipped", "eta": "2026-07-12"}}',
    max_tokens=2_000,
    priority=1,  # freshest information — high priority
)
bundle.add(
    name="conversation_history",
    content="User: Where is my order?\nAssistant: Let me check that for you.",
    max_tokens=6_000,
    priority=3,
)
bundle.add(
    name="input",
    content="User message: Any update on my delivery?",
    max_tokens=4_000,
    priority=0,  # never drop
)

prompt = bundle.assemble()
print(prompt)
Examples
Example 1 — minimal RAG agent context block
This example shows the raw prompt structure a retrieval-augmented agent sends to the model. The XML delimiter pattern keeps source types unambiguous and gives the model clear parsing anchors.

# rag_context_example.py
# Demonstrates how to assemble a minimal RAG context block in Python
# using the Anthropic Claude API.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()  # reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from environment

# Simulated retrieval results (in production, from a vector DB)
retrieved_docs = [
    {"id": "doc-1", "title": "Return Policy", "snippet": "Items must be returned within 30 days of delivery. Opened electronics are not eligible."},
    {"id": "doc-2", "title": "Shipping FAQ", "snippet": "Standard shipping takes 3-5 business days. Express shipping is available at checkout."},
]

def format_retrieval(docs: list[dict]) -> str:
    parts = []
    for doc in docs:
        parts.append(f'<source id="{doc["id"]}" title="{doc["title"]}">\n{doc["snippet"]}\n</source>')
    return "\n".join(parts)

user_question = "Can I return an opened laptop after 45 days?"

messages = [
    {
        "role": "user",
        "content": (
            "Answer using ONLY the reference documents below.\n"
            "If the answer is not clearly supported, respond: \'Not found in provided sources.\'\n\n"
            "<retrieved_context>\n"
            + format_retrieval(retrieved_docs) +
            "\n</retrieved_context>\n\n"
            "<input>\n"
            + user_question +
            "\n</input>"
        ),
    }
]

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-5",
    max_tokens=512,
    system="You are a customer support agent. Use only provided context. Never invent facts.",
    messages=messages,
)

print(response.content[0].text)
# Expected: "Based on the Return Policy, opened electronics are not eligible for return.
#            Additionally, the 30-day return window would have passed after 45 days..."
Example 2 — multi-step agent with tool result injection

A more realistic scenario: an agent calls a tool, receives JSON output, and injects it as structured context for the next reasoning step. This pattern prevents the model from guessing the tool result and ensures fresh API state drives the response.

# tool_result_context.py
# Shows how to inject a tool result as labeled context in a multi-step agent.
# Uses the OpenAI chat completions API with function calling.

import json
import openai

client = openai.OpenAI()  # reads OPENAI_API_KEY from environment

# --- Step 1: model decides to call a tool ---
tools = [
    {
        "type": "function",
        "function": {
            "name": "get_order_status",
            "description": "Look up the current status of a customer order by order ID.",
            "parameters": {
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {"order_id": {"type": "string"}},
                "required": ["order_id"],
            },
        },
    }
]

messages = [
    {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful order-support agent."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Where is order A-991?"},
]

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o",
    tools=tools,
    tool_choice="auto",
    messages=messages,
)

assistant_msg = response.choices[0].message
messages.append(assistant_msg)  # preserve the tool-call message in history

# --- Step 2: execute the tool (simulate API call) ---
def get_order_status(order_id: str) -> dict:
    # In production this would call your order management system
    return {
        "order_id": order_id,
        "status": "shipped",
        "carrier": "FedEx",
        "tracking_number": "7489234898",
        "estimated_delivery": "2026-07-12",
    }

tool_call = assistant_msg.tool_calls[0]
tool_result = get_order_status(json.loads(tool_call.function.arguments)["order_id"])

# --- Step 3: inject tool result as context for the next turn ---
messages.append({
    "role": "tool",
    "tool_call_id": tool_call.id,
    "content": json.dumps(tool_result),  # structured JSON — the model reads this as context
})

# --- Step 4: model reasons using the injected context ---
final_response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o",
    messages=messages,
)

print(final_response.choices[0].message.content)
# Expected: "Order A-991 has shipped via FedEx (tracking: 7489234898).
#            Estimated delivery is July 12, 2026."
Real-world example
A production code-review agent at a mid-size software company ingests pull request diffs and produces inline comments. Its context bundle is structured as follows:
System prompt (static, cached)
— the team's style guide, language-specific rules, and the agent's output format specification. ~4k tokens. Cached at the provider level so it is not re-tokenized on every call.
Retrieval context

— the three most semantically similar past review comments from a vector index of ~50k historical comments. Capped at 6k tokens. Provides few-shot examples of what good reviews look like for this codebase.
Input data
— the diff hunk under review (one file at a time, sliding window of 200 lines max). ~8k tokens. Always placed last for recency bias.

When the team saw the agent missing security issues in authentication code, they initially suspected prompt wording. Investigation showed the retrieval index had not been refreshed after a major refactor six months earlier — retrieved examples showed pre-refactor patterns the model was incorrectly generalizing from. Re-indexing with recent review comments improved security detection without any prompt changes. This illustrates a core principle:
context quality matters more than instruction quality
when factual grounding is the bottleneck.

# code_review_agent.py — simplified production pattern
import anthropic
import tiktoken
from vector_db import VectorStore  # your vector DB abstraction

STYLE_GUIDE = open("docs/style-guide.md").read()  # static, cached in system prompt
enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model("gpt-4o")

def count(text: str) -> int:
    return len(enc.encode(text))

def review_hunk(diff_hunk: str, vector_store: VectorStore) -> str:
    # Retrieve most similar past review comments
    similar_reviews = vector_store.search(diff_hunk, top_k=3)
    retrieval_text = "\n\n".join(
        f"[Past review on {r.file}]\n{r.comment}" for r in similar_reviews
    )

    # Guard on token budget
    if count(retrieval_text) > 6_000:
        # Keep only top-1 if retrieval is too long
        retrieval_text = f"[Past review on {similar_reviews[0].file}]\n{similar_reviews[0].comment}"

    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=f"You are a senior code reviewer.\n\n<style_guide>\n{STYLE_GUIDE}\n</style_guide>",
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": (
                f"<similar_past_reviews>\n{retrieval_text}\n</similar_past_reviews>\n\n"
                f"<diff>\n{diff_hunk}\n</diff>\n\n"
                "Review the diff. Flag bugs, style violations, and security issues."
            ),
        }],
    )
    return response.content[0].text
Common mistakes
Mistake 1 — unlabeled mixed payloads

Pasting different source types into a single undifferentiated text block forces the model to guess where user text ends and API output begins. The model may treat the tool result as a user statement or vice versa.

# BAD — no labels, no delimiters
User asked about their order. {"order_id": "A991", "status": "shipped"} Any update?

# GOOD — labeled sections
<conversation_history>
User: Any update on my order?
</conversation_history>

<tool_result tool="get_order_status" call_id="c-7">
{"order_id": "A991", "status": "shipped", "eta": "2026-07-12"}
</tool_result>

<input>
User follow-up: Can I change the delivery address?
</input>
Mistake 2 — failing to update context after tool calls

A common bug: the agent calls a tool, receives a result, but the next model call reuses the old context bundle from before the tool call. The model answers based on its pre-call assumption rather than the actual result.

# BAD — stale context: tool result not added to message list
messages = [{"role": "user", "content": "What is the weather in Paris?"}]
response1 = call_model(messages)          # model decides to call get_weather
execute_tool(response1.tool_calls[0])     # run the tool
response2 = call_model(messages)          # ← BUG: messages unchanged, no tool result!

# GOOD — always append both the assistant tool-call message and the tool result
messages.append(response1.message)         # assistant: "I'll check the weather"
messages.append({
    "role": "tool",
    "tool_call_id": response1.message.tool_calls[0].id,
    "content": json.dumps(tool_result),   # inject actual weather data
})
response2 = call_model(messages)          # model now sees the real weather data
Mistake 3 — dumping the entire conversation log

Long sessions accumulate hundreds of turns. Injecting the raw full history consumes most of the context budget and buries the current task under old noise. Apply a sliding window (keep last N turns) or a summarization step.

# BAD — inject everything
context = "\n".join(f"{m["role"]}: {m["content"]}" for m in all_messages)

# GOOD — sliding window + summarized older turns
WINDOW = 10
recent = all_messages[-WINDOW:]
if len(all_messages) > WINDOW:
    summary = summarize(all_messages[:-WINDOW])  # compress older history
    context = f"<history_summary>\n{summary}\n</history_summary>\n\n"
    context += "\n".join(f"{m["role"]}: {m["content"]}" for m in recent)
else:
    context = "\n".join(f"{m["role"]}: {m["content"]}" for m in recent)
Mistake 4 — retrieving too many loosely related chunks

Fetching top-20 retrieved chunks with a low similarity threshold floods the context with tangentially related text. The model attends to noise alongside signal and generates vaguer, less accurate answers. Keep top-k small (3–7) and set a minimum similarity threshold.
Best practices
Treat context assembly as production code
— write unit tests asserting that the assembled prompt includes required fields, stays within budget, and has correct delimiters. A broken assembler causes silent quality regressions.
Use stable XML or markdown delimiters consistently
— pick one convention (e.g., XML tags) and apply it everywhere in your product. Inconsistent delimiters degrade the model's ability to locate content.
Place the current input task last
— models exhibit recency bias: the most recent content in the prompt receives the strongest attention. Put the input task and the instruction at the very end.
Label every block with source type, ID, and timestamp
— structured provenance metadata lets the model distinguish user text from API output and lets you audit what the model saw during debugging.
Summarize rather than truncate by default
— truncating mid-sentence creates artifacts and orphaned references. Summarize old history into a compact state object before truncating.
Separate static from dynamic context
— static documents (style guides, schemas) belong in the system prompt where they can be cached. Dynamic content (tool results, retrieved chunks) belongs in the user turn.
Re-fetch context when underlying data changes mid-session
— a cached retrieval result from the start of a long session may be stale by the time a later step needs it. Invalidate and re-fetch when freshness matters.
Never inject raw credentials, keys, or PII into prompts
— prompts are typically logged by model providers. Redact secrets before injection and mask PII to the minimum required for the task.
Performance & scalability

Context length is the single largest cost driver in agentic systems. Input tokens are typically priced at $2–$15 per million tokens depending on model tier. An agent loop running 20 calls with a 16k-token context each consumes 320k tokens of input per user session — at $5/MTok that is $1.60 per session before output tokens. At scale (100k daily active users), unoptimized context assembly can add millions of dollars per month in token cost.
The most impactful optimizations, in rough order of ROI:
Prompt caching for static context

— Anthropic Claude and OpenAI both offer prompt caching (typically 80-90% discount on cached tokens). Place large static documents at the beginning of the system prompt and keep them identical across calls. Savings compound with call volume.
Hierarchical retrieval

— a coarse BM25 or keyword filter narrows the candidate set before the expensive embedding-distance lookup. Reduces retrieval latency and ensures the dense search operates on high-relevance candidates.
Embedding cache
— cache query embeddings for repeated or similar queries. A simple LRU cache with a cosine-similarity dedup threshold prevents redundant embedding API calls.
Structured state object
— maintain a running JSON state object that the orchestrator updates after each step instead of replaying the full conversation. A 500-token state object replaces a 6k-token history block.
Adaptive budget
— reduce retrieval budget on turns where the model is executing a plan step (low information need) and increase it on turns that start a new subtask (high information need).

Latency scales roughly linearly with input length for prefill-dominated models. For latency-sensitive applications, capping the assembled context at the minimum viable size (not the maximum available window) can cut first-token latency by 30–60%.
Tooling & ecosystem
LangChain / LangGraph

— provides ConversationBufferMemory, ConversationSummaryMemory, and ConversationTokenBufferMemory classes that implement sliding-window and summarization strategies. VectorStoreRetriever handles RAG retrieval.
LlamaIndex

— focused on document ingestion and retrieval. ContextChatEngine and CondenseQuestionChatEngine manage context for multi-turn RAG agents. Supports hierarchical retrieval via SummaryIndex + VectorIndex.
Anthropic Claude prompt caching
— add a cache_control breakpoint in the system prompt to tell the API to cache everything before that point. Reduces cost by ~90% for the cached prefix on subsequent calls.
tiktoken / tokenizers
— fast BPE tokenizers for counting prompt tokens before sending to the API. Essential for budget enforcement. tiktoken covers OpenAI models; Anthropic provides anthropic.token_counter.
Mem0
— dedicated agent memory library that maintains a hybrid semantic + entity memory store. Surfaces relevant memories as context chunks on each turn, acting as a managed retrieval layer.
Weaviate / Pinecone / Qdrant
— vector databases for storing and retrieving document embeddings at scale. All three support metadata filtering alongside semantic search, enabling hybrid retrieval pipelines.

# Using Anthropic prompt caching for static context
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

LARGE_POLICY_DOC = open("docs/policy.md").read()  # 10k tokens of static text

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-5",
    max_tokens=1024,
    system=[
        {
            "type": "text",
            "text": "You are a policy assistant.\n\n" + LARGE_POLICY_DOC,
            "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"},  # cache everything above this line
        }
    ],
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What is the refund window for digital products?"}],
)
# On the first call: all tokens counted at full price.
# On subsequent calls: cached prefix billed at ~10% of normal input token price.
print(response.usage)  # inspect cache_read_input_tokens vs cache_creation_input_tokens
Comparison with alternatives
Several architectural patterns exist for giving models access to external knowledge. The comparison below covers the main approaches along axes most relevant to agent engineering.
Approach
How context reaches the model
Freshness
Cost per call
Best for
In-context injection (this topic)
Assembled into each prompt
Real-time
Proportional to context length
Private data, dynamic state, tool results
Fine-tuning
Baked into model weights
Static (training cutoff)
Zero at inference; high training cost
Style, tone, format, fixed domain knowledge
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
Subset of context injection; adds a retrieval step
Index-dependent (minutes to hours)
Retrieval + embedding + context length
Large knowledge bases too big for full injection
Tool/function calling
Model requests; result injected as context
Real-time
Tool latency + context tokens
Structured data, computations, live APIs
External memory (Mem0, MemGPT)
Managed retrieval layer abstracts source selection
Near real-time
Memory query + context tokens
Long-running agents with persistent sessions
Model with large native context (1M+ tokens)
Inject entire corpus
Real-time
Very high (O(n) with input length)
One-shot analysis of single large document
Security & reliability
Context injection is one of the primary attack surfaces for agentic systems.
Prompt injection

attacks embed adversarial instructions inside context sources — a malicious document the agent retrieves, a tool result returned by a compromised API, or a user message designed to override system instructions. Because the model cannot cryptographically distinguish legitimate orchestrator instructions from injected text, defense must be applied at the assembly layer before the prompt is sent.
Key security controls
Never inject unsanitized user-controlled text as system-level context
— user input belongs in the user turn under a clear label, not in the system prompt or mixed with trusted orchestrator context. This limits the blast radius of prompt injection.
Validate and sanitize retrieval results
— scan retrieved document chunks for patterns common in injection attacks (e.g., 'Ignore previous instructions'). Block or redact suspicious content before injection.
Redact credentials and PII before injection
— use a regex or dedicated PII detection library (AWS Comprehend, Presidio) to strip API keys, passwords, SSNs, and email addresses from context blocks.
Log the assembled context for every agent call
— without a log of what the model actually saw, debugging unexpected outputs is nearly impossible. Store context logs with the associated output for at least 30 days in production.
Rate-limit external data sources feeding into context
— a compromised tool or retrieval source that returns arbitrarily large payloads can cause budget exhaustion or context flooding. Enforce per-source token caps at the assembler layer.
Use structured output enforcement for tool results
— validate tool API responses against a schema before injecting them. Malformed or unexpectedly large tool results indicate a failing or compromised external service.
For high-stakes agents (those that can take irreversible actions like sending emails or modifying databases), add a
context verification step

before the final action: a separate model call that reads only the assembled context and the proposed action, then affirms or blocks it. This creates a circuit breaker independent of the main agent's reasoning.
Mental model
Think of the model as a
highly intelligent expert with global amnesia

who is handed a briefing folder before each meeting. The expert has broad world knowledge from their training but cannot remember anything that happened before the current meeting — not previous conversations, not earlier tool calls, not the client's history. The briefing folder (
the assembled context
) is the only thing that bridges the expert's knowledge to the specific situation at hand.

The quality of the expert's contribution depends almost entirely on the quality of that briefing folder. If the folder contains the right documents in the right order, with clear labels and no extraneous noise, the expert will perform brilliantly. If the folder is a dumped mess of unlabeled attachments, outdated memos, and duplicate pages, even the most capable expert will produce confused or incorrect outputs.
The formal model is:
. If a fact is absent from the assembled bundle, it is absent from the model's accessible universe for that call — no amount of prompting can recover information that was never provided.
Key takeaways
The model is stateless
— every call starts from scratch. Context injection is the only mechanism for supplying background knowledge, prior decisions, and real-time data.
Context is assembled code, not a simple string paste
— a well-engineered assembler selects, ranks, budgets, formats, and orders sources deterministically before each inference call.
Label every block with source type and provenance
— unlabeled mixed payloads cause the model to conflate user instructions, API output, and retrieved text, leading to hallucinations and misrouted actions.
Place the current input task last in the prompt
— models exhibit recency bias; the task the model should focus on belongs at the very end of the assembled context.
Refresh context after every tool call
— the most common source of stale reasoning is reusing a context bundle that was assembled before a tool returned its result.
Token budget is a first-class constraint
— define per-source caps, enforce them in code, and summarize or drop low-priority content rather than truncating mid-sentence.
Static context belongs in the system prompt for caching
— large reference documents that do not change per call should be placed where provider-level prompt caching can reduce their token cost by 80–90%.
Context injection is an attack surface
— validate, sanitize, and label all externally sourced content before injection to defend against prompt injection attacks embedded in retrieved documents or tool results.
